title: Nonreciprocal quantum rotation sensing via virtual-excitation enhancement in a spinning cavity
authors: Lu-Qi Yang, Yu-Meng Ren, Peng-Bo Li
abstract: Quantum sensing with high precision and sensitivity plays an important role in quantum technologies and quantum information processing. Here, we propose a nonreciprocal quantum metrological scheme for estimating rotational angular velocity in a hybrid light-matter platform, where the setup consists of a spinning ring cavity coupled to a two-level system and an auxiliary bosonic mode. Through the Sagnac effect, the angular velocity is converted into a direction-dependent detuning, which modifies the effective light-matter dressing of the hybrid system. As a result, the angular velocity is encoded not only into the renormalized hybrid-mode spectrum, but also into the virtual excitations generated by ultrastrong coupling. These virtual excitations modify the polaritonic frequency response to rotation and enhance the quantum Fisher information (QFI) associated with angular velocity estimation, without requiring direct extraction of virtual excitations. Moreover, since the Sagnac-Fizeau shift enters the virtual-transition energy denominators, the metrological response becomes intrinsically different for opposite driving directions, leading to a tunable nonreciprocal sensitivity contrast. In addition, we also discuss a readout scheme and show that bundle emission coincidence counting can serve as an auxiliary direction-dependent readout channel. Our results provide a route toward exploiting nonreciprocal light-matter dressing and virtual excitations as resources for quantum rotation sensing.
summary: The paper details a novel method for highly sensitive rotation sensing using a spinning cavity coupled to matter qubits. By leveraging the Sagnac effect, the angular velocity modifies the system's effective Hamiltonian in a direction-dependent (nonreciprocal) manner. This modification enhances quantum sensitivity by utilizing virtual excitations, allowing for differential readout schemes that surpass standard measurement limits.
main_problem: The paper proposes a nonreciprocal quantum metrological scheme for estimating rotational angular velocity ($\Omega$) with high precision.
main_result: The rotation sensitivity is enhanced by exploiting virtual excitations generated through ultrastrong coupling, leading to an intrinsically nonreciprocal response contrast between opposite driving directions.
method: The analysis uses theoretical techniques including the Schrieffer-Wolff transformation and Bogoliubov diagonalization to derive effective Hamiltonians and calculate the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI).
model_or_system: The system is a hybrid light-matter platform: a spinning ring cavity coupled to a two-level system (TLS) and an auxiliary bosonic mode. The Sagnac effect converts $\Omega$ into a direction-dependent detuning that modifies the effective light-matter dressing.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information ($QFI(\Omega)$), lower-polariton spectral response ($\omega_{-}(\Omega)$), and bundle emission coincidence counting rates.
important_parameters: Angular velocity ($\Omega$), Sagnac-Fizeau shift ($\Delta F(\Omega)$), ultrastrong coupling regime, and the directionality parameter ($s=\pm 1$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes operation in the dispersive regime ($|\Delta_{\pm}(\Omega)| \gg \lambda_{a\sigma}$) and utilizes approximations for QFI based on projecting onto the lower-polariton branch.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the sensing chain ($\Omega 	o \Delta F(\Omega) 	o H_{	ext{eff}} 	o QFI$), showing $	ext{QFI}_{	ext{proxy}}$ landscapes that demonstrate distinct, nonreciprocal distributions for opposite drive directions (RD vs. LD).
paper_structure: The paper systematically derives the effective Hamiltonian using sequential transformations (Schrieffer-Wolff followed by Bogoliubov), calculates the resulting polariton eigenfrequencies and coupling strengths, quantifies sensitivity via QFI, and finally analyzes the nonreciprocal contrast achievable through differential readout.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics as it combines cavity quantum electrodynamics (QED) with advanced sensing techniques. The use of virtual excitations and directional control in a superconducting/optical resonator platform represents a cutting-edge approach for realizing high-precision measurements beyond standard linear response theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the hybrid quantum sensing system. A spinning ring microresonator is coupled to a driven two-level system (TLS, σ) via λaσ and to a bosonic mode o ∈{b, m} via the linearized cou- pling λao. (a) Left drive (LD): The CW optical mode is excited, shifting the cavity resonance to ωa +∆F (Ω). (b) Right drive (RD): The CCW optical mode is excited, shifting the cavity resonance to ωa −∆F (Ω). The two drive directions therefore realize different effective detuning configurations and follow distinct parameter tra- jectories in the SW-Bogoliubov effective Hamiltonian. Depending on the selected resonance condition, bundle emission signals may provide an auxiliary...

FIG. 2. SW-Bogoliubov effective parameter response to the Sagnac- Fizeau shift, with ∆ad/κ = 5.66. (a) The Schrieffer-Wolff kernels K(s) A = g2 R/∆−−g2 CR/∆+ and K(s) χ = 1/∆−−1/∆+ as func- tions of ∆F for LD (solid) and RD (dashed). The plotted kernel values are normalized by κ, i.e., the vertical axes show the dimen- sionless values of the kernels in units of κ−1. The denominators ∆± are defined in Eq. (6). FIG. 4. Directional comparison of the lower-polariton proxy QFI. (a) Line cuts of the normalized proxy QFI F (s) Q,proxy/(4t2|α|2) =

Figure 3 displays the proxy QFI landscapes for the two drive directions on the same physical parameter plane. The opposite Sagnac-Fizeau shifts lead to distinct directional dis- tributions. From the comparison between Fig. 3(a) and (b), the RD branch accesses a broader high-sensitivity region for the representative parameters used here.

Spintronics.note: The paper describes a hybrid light-matter platform (cavity coupled to TLS) and utilizes concepts like nonreciprocity derived from the Sagnac effect, which directly relates to quantum-optics descriptions of spin/photon interactions in structured media.
